text: This is a fifty-one-year-old gentleman who presented with a change in bowel habit. He was found at endoscopy to have a carpet polyp within the rectum, around two-thirds of the circumference of the rectum, from seven centimetres to twelve centimetres. Biopsies indicated this was likely to be a tubovilous adenoma. He was listed for a transanal endoscopic resection, or TEO.
The patient is positioned in the left lateral position. The surgeon uses a diathermy needle to mark the border of the rectal polyp before initiating submucosal dissection.
Interestingly, there were three areas within the polyp where the planes of dissection were less distinct. However, post-operatively these coincided exactly with the points of biopsy.
Fortunately, the histology revealed a large carpet tubovilous adenoma with no evidence of invasion and low-grade dysplasia, which was completely excised.

This gentleman's post-operative recovery was unremarkable and he has thus gone on to endoscopic surveillance. This video shows that although technically challenging, TEO can be used to excise large rectal lesions.
The TEO equipment is shown, featuring a suction irrigator with a metal tip that functions as a monopolar diathermy probe for achieving haemostasis during the procedure.
